business.title: Quote of the day by Meir Statman: "The market may be crazy, but that doesn’t make you a psychiatrist"
business.summary: Behavioral finance expert Meir Statman advises investors against trying to diagnose market volatility, emphasizing that the market's irrationality doesn't require an investor's interpretation. Instead of attempting to predict every twist and turn, focusing on disciplined investing, fundamentals, and risk management proves more effective for long-term success.

business.title: Indian markets capable of absorbing different types of shocks: SEBI chief on West Asia crisis
business.summary: "Due to the prevailing conflict in West Asia, the oil supply chain and its prices got affected in the rest of the world. All the economies have been affected by this and obviously, there are inflationary risks. Besides, spillover effect and second-order effect will also come in," he said.

business.summary: Indian bonds have increasingly tracked U.S. Treasury moves in recent ​years, as higher U.S. yields narrow the return ​premium on emerging-market debt, spur foreign outflows and pressure the rupee.

business.title: Indian firms turn to floating-rate debt as interest rate hikes loom
business.summary: Coupons for floating-rate bonds are priced at a spread over three-month Treasury bill yields and reset quarterly. When rate hikes are expected, these bonds turn more ‌attractive to both ⁠issuers and ⁠investors - companies can borrow at a lower initial cost, while investors benefit from returns that rise over time.
